playground.title: Monday Rover (Raspberry Pi)
playground.cardDescription: Personal project - robotics + computer vision on constrained hardware.
playground.description:
Monday Rover (Raspberry Pi) - Personal project: robotics + computer vision.

What it is: A Raspberry Pi rover built to explore real-time computer vision on constrained hardware.

What I built: Motor control, camera capture, and an on-device vision loop, built with version control, clear module boundaries, and reproducible setup.

What I learned: Constrained hardware forces discipline around latency budgets and reliability under motion.

Honest limitations: This is an exploration, not a product. It is single-purpose and not hardened beyond controlled environments.

playground.title: Generative Video Platform
playground.cardDescription: Personal project - generative AI + product UX for video workflows.
playground.description:
Generative Video Platform - Personal project: generative AI + product UX.

What it is: An exploration of generative AI for video workflows, from prompt UX through platform orchestration.

What I built: An end-to-end prototype including the generation interface, orchestration layer, and handling for slow, expensive, and variable paths.

What I learned: The model is rarely the hard part; product engineering around latency, cost, and output variability is.

Honest limitations: This is a learning prototype, not a launched platform. It is not load-tested or production cost-optimized.
playground.role: Personal project - generative AI + product UX

portfolio.title: Apptio (an IBM company)
portfolio.problem: Migrating financial data pipelines to a higher-performance architecture with zero tolerance for correctness drift.
portfolio.work: Migrated heavy workloads from Kubernetes and Java to EMR Spark with Scala; added a parallel reconciliation pipeline; automated operations with Python, Lambda, and Step Functions; managed infrastructure with Terraform.
portfolio.outcome: Delivered roughly 10x faster processing with zero correctness drift on financial datasets.
portfolio.cardDescription: Financial-data pipeline migration at scale with correctness-critical reconciliation.
portfolio.description:
Apptio (an IBM company) — Senior Software Engineer · 2023–2025. Migrating financial data pipelines to a higher-performance architecture — with zero tolerance for correctness drift.

Context Apptio's data-processing pipelines handled high-volume usage and financial datasets — the kind of data where a customer-facing number being slightly wrong is not a bug, it is a broken product. The pipelines ran on Kubernetes and Java. As volume grew, they were hitting performance limits: some runs stretched across days, and the cost of running them was climbing in step with the data.

The problem The obvious fix — moving the heavy workloads to a faster processing engine — carried a real risk. Financial datasets have zero tolerance for drift. A migration that was 10x faster but introduced even small discrepancies would be worthless, and a big-bang cutover would make any discrepancy hard to isolate. The challenge was not raw performance. It was earning enough confidence in the new system to trust it with money.

What Marwan did He migrated the targeted heavy workloads from Kubernetes and Java to EMR Spark with Scala, chosen for throughput on large datasets. Crucially, he built a parallel reconciliation pipeline: the legacy and new systems ran side by side on the same inputs, and the pipeline flagged any discrepancy between their outputs in real time. That reconciliation harness turned a risky migration into an incremental one — workloads could be cut over a piece at a time, each piece verified against the system it replaced before the old path was retired. Alongside the migration he built automated operational workflows using Python, AWS Lambda, and Step Functions to reduce manual intervention, and used Terraform to provision and manage the AWS environment as code.

The outcome The migration delivered roughly 10x faster processing while maintaining service availability throughout. Most importantly, it completed with zero correctness drift on the financial datasets — the reconciliation pipeline provided the evidence that made an incremental, low-risk cutover possible.

Reflection The reconciliation pipeline was extra work that produced no customer-facing feature — and it was the most important part of the project. It is the clearest example of a principle Marwan applies deliberately: on correctness-critical systems, the migration is the easy half; the proof that the migration is correct is the half that actually matters. Given the same problem again, he would build the reconciliation harness first, before touching the workloads.

portfolio.title: JumpCloud
portfolio.problem: Building a Microsoft M365 integration for a 50,000+ tenant identity platform with conflicting data-shape requirements.
portfolio.work: Directed end-to-end technical design; architected a hybrid data layer with PostgreSQL for identity graph queries and MongoDB for high-volume per-tenant custom attributes; designed multi-tenant GraphQL schema and resolvers.
portfolio.outcome: Shipped to production for 50,000+ tenants while preserving relational performance and absorbing high write volume.
portfolio.cardDescription: M365 integration and hybrid data architecture for a 50,000+ tenant identity platform.
portfolio.description:
JumpCloud — Senior Software Engineer · 2022–2023. A Microsoft M365 integration for an identity platform serving 50,000+ tenants — built on a data layer that had to satisfy two contradictory requirements at once.

Context JumpCloud is a cloud directory platform. Marwan directed the end-to-end technical design of a Microsoft M365 integration that expanded the platform's capabilities and enabled cross-platform compatibility. The integration was exposed through a GraphQL API and had to serve a multi-tenant production environment of more than 50,000 tenants.

The problem The data layer had to do two things that pull in opposite directions. It had to preserve fast, relational queries over the core identity graph — the structured, related data at the heart of a directory product. And it had to absorb a high volume of writes for per-tenant custom attributes and hierarchies, which are flexible, variable, and tenant-specific by nature. A purely relational store would strain under the write volume and the schema variability; a purely document store would give up the relational query performance the identity graph depends on.

What Marwan did He architected a hybrid data layer that used each database for what it is good at. The core identity graph lived in PostgreSQL, keeping relational queries fast and consistent. Per-tenant custom attributes and hierarchies were isolated in MongoDB, where their variability and write volume were a natural fit rather than a fight. On top of this he designed the multi-tenant GraphQL schema and resolvers for the production environment, and delivered the integration end to end using Docker, Golang, gRPC, JavaScript, and Node.js.

The outcome The integration shipped to a production environment of 50,000+ tenants. The hybrid data layer preserved relational query performance on the identity graph while absorbing high write volume across all tenants — the two requirements that were in tension were both met, because each was routed to the store suited to it.

Reflection The instinct under deadline pressure is to pick one database and force the whole problem into it. The better answer here was to recognize that the problem genuinely had two shapes — a relational shape and a document shape — and to stop treating that as something to eliminate. The hybrid layer is more moving parts, but it is the design that stopped fighting the data. Knowing when added structure is worth it is the judgment call, and on a 50,000-tenant system it was.

portfolio.title: Hewlett Packard (HP)
portfolio.problem: Scaling HP Instant Ink from thousands of subscribers to millions on a live, revenue-generating service.
portfolio.work: Planned and led a Domain-Driven Design migration from a Rails monolith to AWS microservices and data pipelines, aligning four distributed teams with clear ownership boundaries.
portfolio.outcome: Scaled to millions of subscribers globally in months while maintaining service reliability.
portfolio.cardDescription: Scaled HP Instant Ink from thousands to millions via DDD-driven cloud migration.
portfolio.description:
Hewlett Packard (HP) — Specialist Software Engineer · 2018–2021. Scaling HP's Instant Ink subscription service from thousands of customers to millions — in months, not years.

Context HP Instant Ink is HP's printer-ink subscription service. The business needed it to grow from serving thousands of customers to serving millions, globally. The system underneath it was a Ruby on Rails monolith — fine at its original scale, but not a foundation that could absorb that kind of growth without buckling.

The problem A monolith does not scale gracefully by orders of magnitude. The work was not a tuning exercise; it was a structural transformation — and it had to happen on a live, revenue-generating service without interrupting customers, and on a timeline measured in months. It also had to happen across four geographically distributed teams, which made clear boundaries and shared architectural direction a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have.

What Marwan did He planned and led the transformation, applying Domain-Driven Design to decompose the Rails monolith into cloud-native microservices and data pipelines on AWS — Lambda, API Gateway, SNS, SQS, DynamoDB, Redshift, CloudFormation. Domain-Driven Design did double duty here: it produced the service boundaries the architecture needed, and it gave four distributed teams a shared language for who owned what. He drove Test-Driven Development and agile practices across those teams, working closely with DevOps, QA, and Product Management to keep the migration stable as it progressed.

The outcome Instant Ink scaled from thousands to millions of subscribers worldwide. The transformation was completed in a matter of months, and service reliability and stability were maintained throughout — customers experienced growth, not disruption.

Reflection The technically interesting part is the microservices migration. The genuinely hard part was coordination: four distributed teams, one live service, a tight clock. Domain-Driven Design is often discussed as a modeling technique — here it was just as much an organizational tool. The boundaries it drew were the same boundaries the teams worked along, which is why the migration could move quickly without the teams colliding.

portfolio.title: AT&T
portfolio.problem: Service-oriented single-page application for ticket and task management.
portfolio.work: Built front-end and back-end components, designed RESTful APIs, developed async JavaScript modules, and created custom query languages for unstructured data; mentored new developers.
portfolio.outcome: Delivered a production SOA application; raised code-quality standards across the team.
portfolio.cardDescription: TQL: a SQL-like query language for ticket search and operational analytics.
portfolio.description:
AT&T — Full Stack Engineer · 2017–2018. TQL: a SQL-like query language for ticket search and operational analytics on a service-oriented single-page application.

Context Support and operations teams needed fast, repeatable ways to search tickets and analyze operational data inside a large SOA environment.

The problem Ad hoc filters and one-off scripts did not scale with ticket volume or with analysts who needed predictable query semantics and bounded execution.

What Marwan did He built front-end and back-end components, designed RESTful APIs, developed async JavaScript modules, and created custom query languages for unstructured data. He mentored new developers and helped raise code-quality standards across the team.

The outcome The team shipped a production SOA application with a usable query surface for real support workflows; grammar, validation, and test coverage were structured for maintainability and debuggable execution paths.

Reflection The language model had to balance expressive queries with predictable semantics — operational usefulness and safety had to move together, not trade off.
